class_schedule.topic: Thinking Geographically: Space, Place, and Scale
learning_objectives: Distinguish between space and place in geographic analysis
Understand geographic scale from local to global
Identify the five themes of geography
readings: de Blij & Muller, Geography: Realms, Regions, and Concepts Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Geography is the study of where things are and why they are there. This course uses geographic thinking to understand cultural diversity across world regions.
key_concepts: Five themes of geography: location, place, human-environment interaction, movement, region
Maps as tools of power and representation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing Mercator and Peters projections to reveal how maps shape our perception of the world

review.value: Location, place, human-environment interaction, movement, and region provide a framework for analyzing any geographic phenomenon
All flat maps distort the globe; different projections preserve different properties (area, shape, distance) and reflect political choices

class_schedule.topic: Culture, Identity, and the Cultural Landscape
learning_objectives: Explain cultural diffusion and its types
Analyze how identity is shaped by geography

lecture_notes.introduction: Culture is the lens through which people interpret their world. Geography shapes culture, and culture transforms geography.
key_concepts: Cultural landscape: the visible imprint of human activity on the land
Cultural diffusion: relocation, expansion, hierarchical, contagious, stimulus
lecture_notes.demonstration: Photo analysis: reading cultural landscapes from images of cities across five continents

review.value: The spread of cultural traits from one place to another through relocation (migration), expansion (spreading outward), or stimulus (idea adaptation)
The visible expression of human activity on the natural landscape, including architecture, agriculture, language signs, and sacred spaces

learning_objectives: Analyze push and pull factors driving migration
Evaluate the challenges of rapid urbanization in the Global South

lecture_notes.introduction: Human population dynamics are reshaping the planet. Where people live, why they move, and how cities grow are central geographic questions.
key_concepts: Push and pull factors of migration
Megacities and urban primacy in developing countries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building and interpreting population pyramids for Japan, Nigeria, and the United States

review.value: The shift from high birth/death rates to low birth/death rates as societies industrialize, passing through stages of population growth
Push factors (poverty, conflict, disaster) drive people away; pull factors (jobs, safety, opportunity) attract them to new locations

class_schedule.topic: Religion, Language, and Ethnicity as Geographic Forces
learning_objectives: Map the global distribution of major religions and language families
Explain how religion and language shape political boundaries
Analyze ethnic conflict through a geographic lens
readings: Lewis, Ethnolinguistic map of the world (reference)

lecture_notes.introduction: Religion and language are among the most powerful markers of cultural identity and have profoundly shaped the political map of the world.
key_concepts: World religions: distribution, diffusion, and landscapes
Language families: Indo-European, Sino-Tibetan, Afro-Asiatic, and others
lecture_notes.demonstration: Overlay exercise: comparing maps of language families, religious majorities, and political borders in Sub-Saharan Africa
setup_requirements: Thematic maps: religion, language, political borders
Case study materials on ethnic conflict

review.value: A common language used for communication between speakers of different native languages, such as English in international business or Swahili in East Africa
The desire of an ethnic group for self-determination and its own state, often arising when ethnic and political boundaries do not align

class_schedule.topic: Political Geography: States, Borders, and Conflict
learning_objectives: Distinguish between states, nations, and nation-states
Analyze different types of political boundaries
Evaluate geographic factors in territorial disputes
readings: Kaplan, The Revenge of Geography Ch. 2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Political geography examines how territory, borders, and spatial organization shape power. The political map is never natural; it is always contested.
key_concepts: Types of boundaries: natural, geometric, ethnic, relict
Boundary disputes: positional, territorial, resource, functional
Geopolitics: Mackinder's Heartland theory and its legacy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: the India-Pakistan border in Kashmir and the Israel-Palestine boundary disputes
setup_requirements: Detailed regional maps of Kashmir and Israel-Palestine

review.value: A nation is a group of people with shared identity; a state is a political entity with sovereignty over territory. They do not always coincide.
Mackinder's geopolitical theory that whoever controls the Eurasian heartland controls the world island and thereby the world

learning_objectives: Analyze the uneven global distribution of natural resources
Evaluate the relationship between geography and environmental vulnerability
Assess geographic dimensions of climate change impacts

lecture_notes.introduction: Environmental challenges are inherently geographic: where resources are, who is most vulnerable, and how human activity reshapes the physical world.
key_concepts: Resource distribution and the resource curse
Climate change: geographic winners and losers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping exercise: identifying climate vulnerability hotspots and overlaying them with development indicators
setup_requirements: GIS mapping software or online tool

review.value: The paradox where countries rich in natural resources often experience slower growth and more conflict than resource-poor countries
The geographic philosophy that the environment sets constraints but human agency determines outcomes, in contrast to environmental determinism

questions.question: Explain the concept of cultural diffusion and distinguish between relocation diffusion and expansion diffusion. Provide a real-world example of each.
questions.answer: Cultural diffusion is the spread of cultural traits from one area to another. Relocation diffusion occurs when people migrate and carry their culture with them (e.g., Chinese immigrants bringing cuisine and festivals to San Francisco's Chinatown). Expansion diffusion occurs when a cultural trait spreads outward from its origin while remaining strong at the source (e.g., the spread of Islam from the Arabian Peninsula across North Africa and Southeast Asia through trade and conquest).

questions.question: What is the demographic transition model? Describe what happens to birth rates and death rates in Stage 2 and explain why.
questions.answer: The demographic transition model describes how populations change as societies industrialize, moving through four stages from high birth/death rates to low birth/death rates. In Stage 2, death rates drop sharply while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth. Death rates fall because of improved sanitation, nutrition, and medicine, while birth rates stay high because cultural norms and economic incentives for large families have not yet changed.

rubric.key: Explains causal factors for the changes

questions.question: Explain the difference between a nation, a state, and a nation-state. Why is the distinction important for understanding political conflict?
questions.answer: A nation is a group of people who share a common identity based on culture, language, history, or ethnicity. A state is a politically organized territory with sovereignty, a government, and recognized borders. A nation-state is the ideal where a nation and state coincide perfectly. The distinction matters because most states contain multiple nations (multinational states) or nations span multiple states (stateless nations like the Kurds), and this mismatch is a primary driver of ethnic conflict, secessionism, and irredentism.

rubric.key: Explains why the distinction matters for conflict

questions.question: What is the 'resource curse' and how does geography help explain it? Give one specific country example.
questions.answer: The resource curse is the paradox where countries with abundant natural resources (especially oil and minerals) often experience slower economic growth, weaker institutions, and more conflict than resource-poor countries. Geography helps explain it because resource location determines who controls wealth, borders drawn through resource-rich areas create disputes, and geographic isolation of extraction sites allows elites to capture rents without broad economic development. Nigeria exemplifies this: despite vast oil reserves in the Niger Delta, the country has experienced endemic corruption, environmental devastation, and insurgency in oil-producing regions.

rubric.key: Provides a specific, accurate country example

major_assignments.title: Regional Cultural Geography Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Select a world region and create a comprehensive geographic and cultural portfolio that analyzes the interplay between physical geography, cultural identity, political organization, and contemporary challenges.
objectives: Apply geographic analysis to a specific world region
Integrate physical and human geography perspectives
Analyze how cultural identity is shaped by geographic context
Evaluate contemporary challenges facing the region
deliverables: Annotated regional map set (physical, political, cultural, economic)
Photo essay with 10 annotated images of the cultural landscape

criteria: Accurate and detailed maps with proper cartographic elements
Thoughtful integration of physical and human geography
Clear spatial analysis of patterns and processes
Appropriate use of geographic terminology
Deep understanding of the region's cultural characteristics
Analysis of how geography shapes cultural identity
Discussion of cultural diversity within the region
Engagement with contemporary cultural dynamics
Minimum 8 scholarly sources
Diverse source types: academic, policy, media
Accurate data and statistics
Proper citation format
Engaging and well-organized presentation
Effective use of visual aids and maps
Demonstrates deep knowledge of the region
Handles questions knowledgeably
Comprehensive geographic and cultural analysis of chosen region
Professional-quality maps and visual materials
Well-researched essay integrating multiple geographic perspectives
Effective presentation demonstrating regional expertise

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on readings and lecture content

participation.description: Active engagement in discussions, mapping exercises, and group activities

major_assignments.description: Regional cultural geography portfolio and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Map-based and essay exam covering world regions and geographic concepts

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam integrating geographic analysis across all regions studied

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower the final grade by one letter step.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 5% per day. Map assignments cannot be submitted late as they are reviewed in class.
course_policies.collaboration: Collaborative mapping exercises are encouraged. All written analysis must be individually authored with proper attribution.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Plagiarism or use of AI to generate submitted essays will result in a zero and academic integrity referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to Google Earth or GIS software, university library databases, and the course LMS.
